guideline_references: NICE NG75 Suspected cancer: recognition and referral (metastatic spinal cord compression)
eviQ / Cancer Council Australia — Malignant spinal cord compression: emergency management
Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Oncological emergencies: MSCC and high-dose dexamethasone
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present home visit where the clinician performed a neurological exam and a digital rectal examination, then administered oral dexamethasone and dispatched a priority ambulance. The AI Doctor CANNOT perform a neuro exam, a DRE, draw a PSA, obtain an MRI, or give dexamethasone — it is scored on recognising a malignant-spinal-cord-compression emergency from PATIENT-REPORTABLE features (weeks of back pain, tonight's acute escalation, new bilateral leg weakness with collapse, weight loss) and escalating to 000 immediately with spinal-immobilisation advice.

Anchoring / premature-closure trap: months of lower urinary tract symptoms the patient attributes to 'getting older' — must NOT be dismissed as benign prostatic hyperplasia when combined with bone pain, weight loss and new neurology.

Over-triage-resistance is NOT the lever here: this is a true patient-reportable oncological emergency (cord compression) — under-triage (treating acute back pain + leg weakness as mechanical/sciatica) is the weighted-3x danger.

No-treatment-as-AI-output: the clinician-administered high-dose dexamethasone and the ED MRI/PSA/decompression pathway are the sealed clinician answer; the AI must escalate, not prescribe steroids or mint a dose.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-18)
